title: Lucidarie
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00140. It encodes the title page and all thirty-five numbered text pages of Friedrich Schmitt's 1909 edition, but not the dissertation's xxix-page German introduction. Schmitt based his text on Cambridge, St John's College, MS G.25, ff. 1r-16r, and collated Cambridge University Library MS Ii.6.26, ff. 79v-101v; the latter is physically defective. The anonymous Middle English Lucidarie, composed or adapted in the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, is kept distinct from the Latin Elucidarium traditionally attributed to Honorius Augustodunensis, the two manuscript witnesses, Schmitt's edition, its complete Google/HathiTrust scan, and the repository's legacy thirty-six-page selection PDF. A complete public-domain edition viewer was verified, but automated whole-volume retrieval was blocked by CAPTCHA or Cloudflare; neither manuscript has a complete public facsimile located as of review. Exact reading-room and reproduction routes are therefore recorded alongside a published partial image of St John's G.25, f. 15r.
review_notes: Middle English is the language of the medieval work. German occurs in Schmitt's title page, editorial notes, and omitted introduction and is not transferred to the work-level language statement.
Lucidarie is treated as an anonymous work-level adaptation. Honorius is associated only with the Latin source work, not assigned as author of the Middle English work.
Schmitt's thirty-four chapter headings and two-book division are editorial source alignment, not manuscript divisions.

entities.label: Lucidarie
entities.description: Anonymous Middle English prose adaptation of part of the Latin Elucidarium, with substantial omissions, rearrangement, and fourteen added question-and-answer units.

date_statements.label: composition or adaptation
date_statements.value: late fourteenth or early fifteenth century

notes: Lewis argues that calling the work a Lollard text is misleading: it combines orthodox and potentially heterodox positions and may have been influenced by Lollard ideas without being safely classified as Lollard.
Neither surviving copy identifies a Latin source or acknowledges translation.

entities.label: Elucidarium
entities.description: Three-book Latin theological dialogue traditionally attributed to Honorius Augustodunensis and widely translated and adapted in medieval Europe.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.value: late eleventh or early twelfth century; possibly as early as 1096

notes: Honorius is retained as the traditional or attributed Latin author. The physical Latin exemplar used by the Middle English adapter has not been identified.

entities.label: Cambridge, St John's College, MS G.25

date_statements.label: manuscript date
date_statements.value: first half of the fifteenth century

holding.notes: No stable current item-level public catalog URL was located; the generic library and exact request route are recorded under access.

notes: Twenty-nine and twenty-eight lines per page in the codex description; the first two quires containing the Lucidarie are written by Hand A in textura semi-quadrata.
Sarah James reports that Hand A is localized by LALME to Northamptonshire; this is a witness-scribe dialect localization, not a place of composition.
Schmitt's base witness and the more careful and more complete of the two copies.
James calls the ending on f. 16r unfinished; the preserved final master response is syntactically complete but lacks a formal explicit.

entities.label: Cambridge, University Library, MS Ii.6.26

holding.notes: The Apollo item is a transcription of an unpublished M. R. James description, not a complete current catalog or manuscript facsimile.

physical_description.extent: 103 leaves; the collation separately notes two flyleaves and a pastedown; Lucistrye on ff. 79v-101v
notes: Twenty-five to twenty-six lines per page; clear black handwriting with a slight slope; paper boards.

The codex is physically defective. James's collation records a major gap and final quire losses; Lewis describes as many as seven quires missing between ff. 88 and 89 and a possible further loss between ff. 96 and 97.
The Lucistrye follows twelve tracts favoring vernacular scripture. It is a rougher copy than J and has extensive physical lacunae.

agents.name: Honorius Augustodunensis
notes: Traditional or attributed author of the Latin Elucidarium; not author of the Middle English Lucidarie. Marx notes that the association with Autun has been rejected.

rights.component: Kyvero Press repository code and software under the pinned root LICENSE

rights.component: medieval Middle English text as an abstract work
rights.basis: The work was composed around the turn of the fifteenth century and its anonymous creator died centuries before modern copyright terms.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, or photography.

rights.basis: HathiTrust assigns item uc1.b2644788 rights code pdus and the string US Full View; Google Books labels the corresponding volume public domain and full view.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations in J and U
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded letters printed in italics and retained as HI REND=italic in the XML.
editorial_practices.description: Schmitt expands manuscript abbreviations and signals supplied letters typographically; the encoding preserves this intervention with 847 HI elements.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English edited text on pp. 1-35

editorial_practices.representation: Modern punctuation introduced by the editor.
editorial_practices.description: Schmitt states that he supplied modern punctuation rather than reproducing the manuscripts' punctuation diplomatically.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English edited text on pp. 1-35

editorial_practices.description: Schmitt reports generally using lower case rather than preserving every manuscript capital.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English edited text on pp. 1-35

editorial_practices.source_feature: J's rubricated master and disciple tags and red-underlined biblical passages
editorial_practices.representation: Rubricated tags represented with letterspacing; scriptural citations signaled typographically and by editorial references.
editorial_practices.description: Schmitt translates selected color and emphasis features of J into monochrome typography rather than color facsimile.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Variant readings, Latin quotations, and lacunae in U
editorial_practices.representation: All U variants in the first three chapters and selected significant variants thereafter, printed as German apparatus notes.
editorial_practices.description: Schmitt uses J as base and U as a comparison witness; CME00140 retains this apparatus as 494 NOTE elements and all U and J milestone references present in the selected pages.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp. 1-35; J ff. 1r-16r and U ff. 79v-101v where extant

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete Schmitt dissertation and edition, xxix + 35 pages
editorial_practices.representation: Title page and numbered edition pp. 1-35 encoded; pp. i-xxix omitted.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding is complete for Schmitt's printed Middle English text and title page but is not a complete transcription of the 1909 publication.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire U-M encoding and repository XML
notes: The XML's two books and thirty-four chapters preserve Schmitt's imposed alignment with the Latin source tradition; neither manuscript has those divisions.

evidence.citation: Anna Lewis, 'Rethinking the Lollardy of the Lucidarie: The Middle English Version of the Elucidarium and Religious Thought in Late Medieval England,' Florilegium 27 (2010): 209-236.
evidence.summary: Establishes title usage, work date, two witnesses and loci, common-exemplar hypothesis, incomplete adaptation structure, added questions, manuscript contexts, J's relative completeness, U's lacunae, the lack of manuscript book/chapter divisions, and the argument that the Lollard label is misleading.

evidence.citation: C. W. Marx, 'An Abbreviated Middle English Prose translation of the Elucidarius,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 31 (2000): 1-54.
evidence.locator: pp. 1-54, especially introduction and comparative Table 2
evidence.summary: Provides Latin Elucidarium context and a detailed crosswalk for Schmitt's Middle English version, including selective Book I coverage, Book II.8 and II.3-4, 6-7, and fourteen added question-and-answer units.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of St John's College, Cambridge (1913), no. 193.

evidence.summary: Describes G.25 as membrane, approximately 195 x 130 mm, first half of the fifteenth century, with two textura semi-quadrata hands; Hand A copied the first two quires containing only the Elucidarium and is localized to Northamptonshire. The repository supplies the complete accepted manuscript as a Word document.

open_questions.question: Why does Lewis state that the Lucidarie includes six questions from Latin Book II when her following detailed sequence and Marx's Table 2 identify II.8 plus II.3-4, 6-7, a set of five numbered Latin questions?
next_steps: Collate every Middle English exchange in Schmitt pp. 29-35 against the numbered questions in Lefevre's Latin edition.
Check whether Lewis counts a split, compound, or differently numbered Latin question that Marx's table groups differently.
Use 'a small set of Book II questions' in summary fields until the numbering discrepancy is resolved.

open_questions.question: Can a complete local research copy of Schmitt's xxix + 35-page edition be obtained from the verified Google Books or HathiTrust public-domain routes without bypassing access controls?
next_steps: Attempt the Google Books PDF download interactively in a normal browser and preserve the exact provider-delivered file if available.
Ask a HathiTrust member-library user to test the Full View download controls for uc1.b2644788.
Keep the thirty-six-page legacy selection explicitly partial until the complete provider object is cached.
notes: Automated Google downloads redirected to CAPTCHA and HathiTrust browser access returned Cloudflare HTTP 403.

open_questions.question: Does St John's College have a complete digitization or stable item-level catalog record for MS G.25 that is not publicly indexed?
next_steps: Email special.collections@joh.cam.ac.uk with shelfmark G.25 and request the catalog record and complete-facsimile status.
If images are supplied, cache the complete codex or complete work-bearing quires according to repository permission and record exact coverage.
Do not treat Lewis Figure 1, f. 15r, as more than a one-folio reproduction.

open_questions.question: Does Cambridge University Library have a complete digital facsimile or current item-level catalog record for MS Ii.6.26 beyond the public M. R. James description?
next_steps: Ask mss@lib.cam.ac.uk for the current catalog record, digitization status, and imaging options.
Request a Virtual Reading Room review of ff. 79v-101v and the physical gaps if a full image order is not feasible.
Preserve any complete provider object and document the exact folio-to-image crosswalk.

open_questions.question: Why does James call the G.25 Lucidarie ending 'unfinished' when the preserved final master response on f. 16r is syntactically complete and is treated by Schmitt and Lewis as the more complete copy?
next_steps: Inspect f. 16r-16v directly for ruling, quire construction, erased text, catchwords, and evidence of loss.
Compare the ending with U, the Latin source sequence, and other Middle English Elucidarium adaptations.
Distinguish absence of a formal explicit from loss of intended textual matter.

open_questions.question: How do Schmitt's internal-page lacuna statements for U relate exactly to the quire losses and present foliation described by James and Lewis?
next_steps: Collate Schmitt's U page milestones 158-202 and apparatus notes against images of ff. 79v-101v.
Reconstruct the codex collation around present ff. 88/89 and 96/97 without assuming that later page numbers map continuously across missing quires.
Record separate physical, paginal, and textual lacuna intervals.

open_questions.question: Which Latin Elucidarium recension or physical exemplar underlies the anonymous Middle English adaptation?
next_steps: Collate the Middle English readings and sequence against Lefevre's Latin edition and current Elucidarium stemmatic scholarship.
Separate direct translation, paraphrase, omitted questions, rearranged questions, and the fourteen additions.
Do not identify either surviving Middle English witness or Schmitt's printed Latin alignment as the adapter's physical source.
